Title: Depth-First Search (DFS)
Description: A graph traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking, using a stack (explicit or call stack) to track the path.
Explanation:
Depth-First Search (DFS) is a fundamental graph traversal algorithm. Starting from a source node, it explores as **deep as possible** along each branch before backtracking to explore other branches.

**How it works:**
- Start at the source node, mark it as visited.
- Explore one of its unvisited neighbors — go as deep as possible.
- When a node has no unvisited neighbors, **backtrack** to the previous node.
- Repeat until all reachable nodes are visited.

**Two implementations:**
- **Recursive:** Uses the program's call stack implicitly. Clean and concise.
- **Iterative:** Uses an explicit Stack data structure. Avoids stack overflow for very deep graphs.

**DFS supports the following operations:**

**Recursive DFS:** Traverse all reachable nodes from a source, printing them in DFS order.

**Iterative DFS:** Same traversal using an explicit stack instead of recursion.

**Detect Cycle:** In an undirected graph, a cycle exists if DFS visits a node that is already in the current recursion stack (visited and not the parent).

**Find Path:** Use DFS to find any path between a source and a destination node. Track the path using a list and backtrack when needed.

**Count Connected Components:** Run DFS from every unvisited node. Each new DFS call starts a new connected component.

**Graph representation used — Adjacency List:**
Each node maps to a list of its neighbors. Space efficient for sparse graphs — O(V + E) space.

**Time & Space Complexity:**
- Time: O(V + E) — visits every vertex and edge once.
- Space: O(V) — for the visited set and recursion stack.

**DFS vs BFS:**
- DFS goes deep first — good for cycle detection, path finding, topological sort.
- BFS goes wide first — good for shortest path in unweighted graphs.
